From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Digital music](/w/index.php?title=Digital_music&redirect=no "Digital music"))

Technology that records, stores, and reproduces sound

"Digital Audio" redirects here; not to be confused with [*Digital Audio* (magazine)](/wiki/Digital_Audio_(magazine) "Digital Audio (magazine)").

"Digital music" redirects here. For modern music composed by digital or electronic means, see [Computer music](/wiki/Computer_music "Computer music") and [Electronic music](/wiki/Electronic_music "Electronic music").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Zoom_H4n_audio_recording_levels.jpg/250px-Zoom_H4n_audio_recording_levels.jpg)](/wiki/File:Zoom_H4n_audio_recording_levels.jpg)

Audio levels display on a digital audio recorder ([Zoom H4n](/wiki/Zoom_H4n "Zoom H4n"))

**Digital audio** is a representation of sound recorded in, or converted into, [digital form](/wiki/Digital_signal_(signal_processing) "Digital signal (signal processing)"). In digital audio, the [sound wave](/wiki/Sound_wave "Sound wave") of the [audio signal](/wiki/Audio_signal "Audio signal") is typically encoded as numerical [samples](/wiki/Sampling_(signal_processing) "Sampling (signal processing)") in a continuous sequence. For example, in [CD audio](/wiki/CD_audio "CD audio"), [samples](/wiki/Sampling_(signal_processing)#Audio_sampling "Sampling (signal processing)") are taken 44,100 [times per second](/wiki/Hertz#Sound_and_vibration "Hertz"), each with 16-bit [resolution](/wiki/Audio_bit_depth "Audio bit depth"). Digital audio is also the name for the entire technology of [sound recording and reproduction](/wiki/Sound_recording_and_reproduction "Sound recording and reproduction") using audio signals that have been encoded in digital form. Following significant advances in digital audio technology during the 1970s and 1980s, it gradually replaced [analog audio technology](/wiki/Comparison_of_analog_and_digital_recording "Comparison of analog and digital recording") in many areas of [audio engineering](/wiki/Audio_engineering "Audio engineering"), [record production](/wiki/Record_production "Record production") and [telecommunications](/wiki/Telecommunications "Telecommunications") in the 1990s and 2000s.

In a digital audio system, an [analog electrical signal](/wiki/Analog_signal "Analog signal") representing the sound is converted with an [analog-to-digital converter](/wiki/Analog-to-digital_converter "Analog-to-digital converter") (ADC) into a digital signal, typically using [pulse-code modulation](/wiki/Pulse-code_modulation "Pulse-code modulation") (PCM). This digital signal can then be recorded, edited, modified, and copied using [computers](/wiki/Computer "Computer"), audio playback machines, and other digital tools. For playback, a [digital-to-analog converter](/wiki/Digital-to-analog_converter "Digital-to-analog converter") (DAC) performs the reverse process, converting a digital signal back into an analog signal, which is then sent through an [audio power amplifier](/wiki/Audio_power_amplifier "Audio power amplifier") and ultimately to a [loudspeaker](/wiki/Loudspeaker "Loudspeaker").

Digital audio systems may include [compression](/wiki/Audio_compression_(data) "Audio compression (data)"), [storage](/wiki/Computer_data_storage "Computer data storage"), [processing](/wiki/Digital_signal_processing "Digital signal processing"), and [transmission](/wiki/Data_transmission "Data transmission") components. Conversion to a digital format allows convenient manipulation, storage, transmission, and retrieval of an audio signal. Unlike analog audio, in which making copies of a recording results in [generation loss](/wiki/Generation_loss "Generation loss") and degradation of signal quality, digital audio allows an infinite number of copies to be made without any degradation of signal quality.

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Digital_audio&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/4-bit-linear-PCM.svg/330px-4-bit-linear-PCM.svg.png)](/wiki/File:4-bit-linear-PCM.svg)

A sound wave, in red, represented digitally, in blue (after [sampling](/wiki/Sampling_(signal_processing) "Sampling (signal processing)") and 4-bit [quantization](/wiki/Quantization_(signal_processing) "Quantization (signal processing)")).

Digital audio technologies are used in the recording, manipulation, mass-production, and distribution of sound, including recordings of [songs](/wiki/Song "Song"), instrumental pieces, [podcasts](/wiki/Podcast "Podcast"), sound effects, and other sounds. Modern [online music distribution](/wiki/Music_download "Music download") depends on digital recording and [data compression](/wiki/Audio_compression_(data) "Audio compression (data)"). The availability of music as data files, rather than as physical objects, has significantly reduced the costs of distribution as well as making it easier to share copies.[[1]](#cite_note-Janssens-1) Before digital audio, the music industry distributed and sold music by selling physical copies in the form of [records](/wiki/Phonograph_record "Phonograph record") and [cassette tapes](/wiki/Cassette_tape "Cassette tape"). With digital audio and online distribution systems such as [iTunes](/wiki/ITunes "ITunes"), companies sell digital sound files to consumers, which the consumer receives over the Internet. Popular streaming services such as [Apple Music](/wiki/Apple_Music "Apple Music"), [Spotify](/wiki/Spotify "Spotify"), or [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube"), offer temporary access to the digital file, and are now the most common form of music consumption.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

An analog audio system converts physical waveforms of sound into electrical representations of those waveforms by use of a [transducer](/wiki/Transducer "Transducer"), such as a [microphone](/wiki/Microphone "Microphone"). The sounds are then stored on an analog medium such as [magnetic tape](/wiki/Magnetic_tape "Magnetic tape"), or transmitted through an analog medium such as a [telephone line](/wiki/Telephone_line "Telephone line") or [radio](/wiki/Radio_broadcasting "Radio broadcasting"). The process is reversed for reproduction: the electrical audio signal is [amplified](/wiki/Amplifier "Amplifier") and then converted back into physical waveforms via a [loudspeaker](/wiki/Loudspeaker "Loudspeaker"). Analog audio retains its fundamental wave-like characteristics throughout its storage, transformation, duplication, and amplification.

[Analog audio signals](/wiki/Analog_signal "Analog signal") are susceptible to noise and distortion, due to the innate characteristics of electronic circuits and associated devices. Disturbances in a [digital system](/wiki/Digital_system "Digital system") do not result in error unless they are so large as to result in a symbol being misinterpreted as another symbol or disturbing the sequence of symbols. It is, therefore, generally possible to have an entirely error-free digital audio system in which no noise or distortion is introduced between conversion to digital format and conversion back to analog.[[a]](#cite_note-4)

A digital audio signal may be encoded for correction of any errors that might occur in the storage or transmission of the signal. This technique, known as [channel coding](/wiki/Channel_coding "Channel coding"), is essential for broadcast or recorded digital systems to maintain bit accuracy. [Eight-to-fourteen modulation](/wiki/Eight-to-fourteen_modulation "Eight-to-fourteen modulation") is the channel code used for the audio [compact disc](/wiki/Compact_disc "Compact disc") (CD).

### Conversion process

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Digital_audio&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Conversion process")]

[![Analog to Digital to Analog conversion](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/A-D-A_Flow.svg/250px-A-D-A_Flow.svg.png)](/wiki/File:A-D-A_Flow.svg)

The lifecycle of sound from its source, through an ADC, digital processing, a DAC, and finally as sound again.

If an audio signal is analog, a digital audio system starts with an ADC that converts an analog signal to a digital signal.[[b]](#cite_note-5) The ADC runs at a specified [sampling rate](/wiki/Sampling_rate "Sampling rate") and converts at a known bit resolution. [CD audio](/wiki/CD_audio "CD audio"), for example, has a sampling rate of 44.1 [kHz](/wiki/KHz "KHz") (44,100 samples per second), and has 16-bit [resolution](/wiki/Audio_bit_depth "Audio bit depth") for each [stereo](/wiki/Stereo "Stereo") channel. Analog signals that have not already been [bandlimited](/wiki/Bandlimited "Bandlimited") must be passed through an [anti-aliasing filter](/wiki/Anti-aliasing_filter "Anti-aliasing filter") before conversion, to prevent the [aliasing distortion](/wiki/Aliasing "Aliasing") that is caused by audio signals with frequencies higher than the [Nyquist frequency](/wiki/Nyquist_frequency "Nyquist frequency") (half the sampling rate).

A digital audio signal may be stored or transmitted. Digital audio can be stored on a CD, a [digital audio player](/wiki/Digital_audio_player "Digital audio player"), a [hard drive](/wiki/Hard_drive "Hard drive"), a [USB flash drive](/wiki/USB_flash_drive "USB flash drive"), or any other digital [data storage device](/wiki/Data_storage_device "Data storage device"). The digital signal may be altered through [digital signal processing](/wiki/Digital_signal_processing "Digital signal processing"), where it may be [filtered](/wiki/Audio_filter "Audio filter") or have [effects](/wiki/Audio_signal_processing "Audio signal processing") applied. [Sample-rate conversion](/wiki/Sample-rate_conversion "Sample-rate conversion"), including [upsampling](/wiki/Upsampling "Upsampling") and [downsampling](/wiki/Downsampling "Downsampling"), may be used to change signals that have been encoded with a different sampling rate to a common sampling rate prior to processing. Audio data compression techniques, such as [MP3](/wiki/MP3 "MP3"), [Advanced Audio Coding](/wiki/Advanced_Audio_Coding "Advanced Audio Coding") (AAC), [Opus](/wiki/Opus_(audio_format) "Opus (audio format)"), [Ogg Vorbis](/wiki/Ogg_Vorbis "Ogg Vorbis"), or [FLAC](/wiki/FLAC "FLAC"), are commonly employed to reduce the file size. Digital audio can be carried over [digital audio interfaces](/wiki/Digital_audio_interface "Digital audio interface") such as [AES3](/wiki/AES3 "AES3") or [MADI](/wiki/MADI "MADI"). Digital audio can be carried over a network using [audio over Ethernet](/wiki/Audio_over_Ethernet "Audio over Ethernet"), [audio over IP](/wiki/Audio_over_IP "Audio over IP") or other [streaming media](/wiki/Streaming_media "Streaming media") standards and systems.

For playback, digital audio must be converted back to an analog signal with a DAC. According to the [Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem](/wiki/Nyquist%E2%80%93Shannon_sampling_theorem "Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem"), with some practical and theoretical restrictions, a band-limited version of the original analog signal can be accurately reconstructed from the digital signal.

During conversion, audio data can be embedded with a [digital watermark](/wiki/Digital_watermark "Digital watermark") to prevent piracy and unauthorized use. Watermarking is done using a [direct-sequence spread-spectrum](/wiki/Direct-sequence_spread-spectrum "Direct-sequence spread-spectrum") (DSSS) method. The audio information is then modulated by a [pseudo-noise](/wiki/Pseudo-noise "Pseudo-noise") (PN) sequence, then shaped within the frequency domain and put back in the original signal. The strength of the embedding determines the strength of the watermark on the audio data.[[4]](#cite_note-6)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Digital_audio&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

### Coding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Digital_audio&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Coding")]

Main articles: [Audio coding format](/wiki/Audio_coding_format "Audio coding format") and [Audio data compression](/wiki/Audio_data_compression "Audio data compression")

[Pulse-code modulation](/wiki/Pulse-code_modulation "Pulse-code modulation") (PCM) was invented by British scientist [Alec Reeves](/wiki/Alec_Reeves "Alec Reeves") in 1937.[[5]](#cite_note-7) In 1950, [C. Chapin Cutler](/wiki/C._Chapin_Cutler "C. Chapin Cutler") of [Bell Labs](/wiki/Bell_Labs "Bell Labs") filed the patent on [differential pulse-code modulation](/wiki/Differential_pulse-code_modulation "Differential pulse-code modulation") (DPCM),[[6]](#cite_note-DPCM-8) a [data compression](/wiki/Data_compression "Data compression") algorithm. [Adaptive DPCM](/wiki/Adaptive_DPCM "Adaptive DPCM") (ADPCM) was introduced by P. Cummiskey, [Nikil S. Jayant](/wiki/Nikil_Jayant "Nikil Jayant") and [James L. Flanagan](/wiki/James_L._Flanagan "James L. Flanagan") at Bell Labs in 1973.[[7]](#cite_note-9)[[8]](#cite_note-10)

[Perceptual coding](/wiki/Perceptual_coding "Perceptual coding") was first used for [speech coding](/wiki/Speech_coding "Speech coding") compression, with [linear predictive coding](/wiki/Linear_predictive_coding "Linear predictive coding") (LPC).[[9]](#cite_note-Schroeder2014-11) Initial concepts for LPC date back to the work of [Fumitada Itakura](/wiki/Fumitada_Itakura "Fumitada Itakura") ([Nagoya University](/wiki/Nagoya_University "Nagoya University")) and Shuzo Saito ([Nippon Telegraph and Telephone](/wiki/Nippon_Telegraph_and_Telephone "Nippon Telegraph and Telephone")) in 1966.[[10]](#cite_note-12) During the 1970s, [Bishnu S. Atal](/wiki/Bishnu_S._Atal "Bishnu S. Atal") and [Manfred R. Schroeder](/wiki/Manfred_R._Schroeder "Manfred R. Schroeder") at Bell Labs developed a form of LPC called [adaptive predictive coding](/wiki/Adaptive_predictive_coding "Adaptive predictive coding") (APC), a perceptual coding algorithm that exploited the masking properties of the human ear, followed in the early 1980s with the [code-excited linear prediction](/wiki/Code-excited_linear_prediction "Code-excited linear prediction") (CELP) algorithm.[[9]](#cite_note-Schroeder2014-11)

[Discrete cosine transform](/wiki/Discrete_cosine_transform "Discrete cosine transform") (DCT) coding, a [lossy compression](/wiki/Lossy_compression "Lossy compression") method first proposed by [Nasir Ahmed](/wiki/Nasir_Ahmed_(engineer) "Nasir Ahmed (engineer)") in 1972,[[11]](#cite_note-Ahmed-13)[[12]](#cite_note-DCT-14) provided the basis for the [modified discrete cosine transform](/wiki/Modified_discrete_cosine_transform "Modified discrete cosine transform") (MDCT), which was developed by J. P. Princen, A. W. Johnson and A. B. Bradley in 1987.[[13]](#cite_note-15) The MDCT is the basis for most [audio coding standards](/wiki/Audio_coding_standards "Audio coding standards"), such as [Dolby Digital](/wiki/Dolby_Digital "Dolby Digital") (AC-3),[[14]](#cite_note-Luo-16) MP3 ([MPEG](/wiki/MPEG "MPEG") Layer III),[[15]](#cite_note-Guckert-17)[[9]](#cite_note-Schroeder2014-11) AAC, [Windows Media Audio](/wiki/Windows_Media_Audio "Windows Media Audio") (WMA), Opus and [Vorbis](/wiki/Vorbis "Vorbis") ([Ogg](/wiki/Ogg "Ogg")).[[14]](#cite_note-Luo-16)

### Recording

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Digital_audio&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Recording")]

Main article: [Digital recording](/wiki/Digital_recording "Digital recording")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/REAPER_Digital_Audio_Workstation.jpg/250px-REAPER_Digital_Audio_Workstation.jpg)](/wiki/File:REAPER_Digital_Audio_Workstation.jpg)

[Digital audio workstation](/wiki/Digital_audio_workstation "Digital audio workstation")

PCM was used in [telecommunications](/wiki/Telecommunications "Telecommunications") applications long before its first use in commercial broadcast and recording. Commercial digital recording was pioneered in Japan by [NHK](/wiki/NHK "NHK") and [Nippon Columbia](/wiki/Nippon_Columbia "Nippon Columbia") and their [Denon](/wiki/Denon "Denon") brand, in the 1960s. The first commercial digital recordings were released in 1971.[[16]](#cite_note-Fine-18)

The [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") also began to experiment with digital audio in the 1960s. By the early 1970s, it had developed a 2-channel recorder, and in 1972 it deployed a digital audio transmission system that linked its broadcast center to its remote transmitters.[[16]](#cite_note-Fine-18)

The first 16-bit PCM recording in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") was made by [Thomas Stockham](/wiki/Thomas_Stockham "Thomas Stockham") at the [Santa Fe Opera](/wiki/Santa_Fe_Opera "Santa Fe Opera") in 1976, on a [Soundstream](/wiki/Soundstream "Soundstream") recorder. An improved version of the Soundstream system was used to produce several classical recordings by [Telarc](/wiki/Telarc "Telarc") in 1978. The [3M](/wiki/3M "3M") digital [multitrack recorder](/wiki/Multitrack_recorder "Multitrack recorder") in development at the time was based on BBC technology. The first all-digital album recorded on this machine was [Ry Cooder](/wiki/Ry_Cooder "Ry Cooder")'s *[Bop till You Drop](/wiki/Bop_till_You_Drop "Bop till You Drop")* in 1979. British record label [Decca](/wiki/Decca_Records "Decca Records") began development of its own 2-track digital audio recorders in 1978 and released the first European digital recording in 1979.[[16]](#cite_note-Fine-18)

Popular professional digital multitrack recorders produced by Sony/Studer ([DASH](/wiki/Digital_Audio_Stationary_Head "Digital Audio Stationary Head")) and Mitsubishi ([ProDigi](/wiki/ProDigi "ProDigi")) in the early 1980s helped to bring about digital recording's acceptance by the major record companies. Machines for these formats had their own transports built in as well, using [reel-to-reel](/wiki/Reel-to-reel "Reel-to-reel") tape in either 1/4, 1/2, or 1-inch widths, with the audio data being recorded to the tape using a multi-track stationary tape head. [PCM adaptors](/wiki/PCM_adaptor "PCM adaptor") allowed for stereo digital audio recording on a conventional NTSC or PAL [video tape recorder](/wiki/Video_tape_recorder "Video tape recorder").

The 1982 introduction of the CD by [Philips](/wiki/Philips "Philips") and [Sony](/wiki/Sony "Sony") popularized digital audio with consumers.[[16]](#cite_note-Fine-18)

[ADAT](/wiki/ADAT "ADAT") became available in the early 1990s, which allowed eight-track [44.1](/wiki/44,100_Hz "44,100 Hz") or [48 kHz](/wiki/48,000_Hz "48,000 Hz") recording on S-VHS cassettes, and [DTRS](/wiki/DTRS "DTRS") performed a similar function with Hi8 tapes.

Formats like ProDigi and DASH were referred to as **SDAT** (stationary-head digital audio tape) formats, as opposed to formats like the PCM adaptor-based systems and [Digital Audio Tape](/wiki/Digital_Audio_Tape "Digital Audio Tape") (DAT), which were referred to as **RDAT** (rotating-head digital audio tape) formats, due to their helical-scan process of recording.

Like the DAT cassette, ProDigi and DASH machines also accommodated the obligatory 44.1 kHz sampling rate, but also 48 kHz on all machines, and eventually a 96 kHz sampling rate. They overcame the problems that made typical analog recorders unable to meet the bandwidth (frequency range) demands of digital recording by a combination of higher tape speeds, narrower head gaps used in combination with metal-formulation tapes, and the spreading of data across multiple parallel tracks.

Unlike analog systems, modern [digital audio workstations](/wiki/Digital_audio_workstation "Digital audio workstation") and [audio interfaces](/wiki/Audio_interface "Audio interface") allow as many channels in as many different sampling rates as the computer can effectively run at a single time. [Avid Audio](/wiki/Avid_Audio "Avid Audio") and [Steinberg](/wiki/Steinberg "Steinberg") released the first digital audio workstation software programs in 1989.[[17]](#cite_note-:0-19) Digital audio workstations make multitrack recording and mixing much easier for large projects, which would otherwise be difficult with analog equipment.

### Telephony

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Digital_audio&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Telephony")]

Main article: [Digital telephony](/wiki/Digital_telephony "Digital telephony")

The rapid development and wide adoption of PCM [digital telephony](/wiki/Digital_telephony "Digital telephony") was enabled by [metal–oxide–semiconductor](/wiki/Metal%E2%80%93oxide%E2%80%93semiconductor "Metal–oxide–semiconductor") (MOS) [switched capacitor](/wiki/Switched_capacitor "Switched capacitor") (SC) circuit technology, developed in the early 1970s.[[18]](#cite_note-Allstot-20) This led to the development of PCM codec-filter chips in the late 1970s.[[18]](#cite_note-Allstot-20)[[19]](#cite_note-Gibson26-21) The [silicon-gate](/wiki/Silicon-gate "Silicon-gate") [CMOS](/wiki/CMOS "CMOS") (complementary MOS) PCM codec-filter chip, developed by [David A. Hodges](/wiki/David_A._Hodges "David A. Hodges") and W.C. Black in 1980,[[18]](#cite_note-Allstot-20) has since been the industry standard for digital telephony.[[18]](#cite_note-Allstot-20)[[19]](#cite_note-Gibson26-21) By the 1990s, [telecommunication networks](/wiki/Telecommunication_network "Telecommunication network") such as the [public switched telephone network](/wiki/Public_switched_telephone_network "Public switched telephone network") (PSTN) had been largely [digitized](/wiki/Digitized "Digitized") with [VLSI](/wiki/VLSI "VLSI") (very [large-scale integration](/wiki/Large-scale_integration "Large-scale integration")) CMOS PCM codec-filters, widely used in [electronic switching systems](/wiki/Electronic_switching_system "Electronic switching system") for [telephone exchanges](/wiki/Telephone_exchanges "Telephone exchanges"), user-end [modems](/wiki/Modems "Modems") and a range of [digital transmission](/wiki/Digital_transmission "Digital transmission") applications such as the [integrated services digital network](/wiki/Integrated_services_digital_network "Integrated services digital network") (ISDN), [cordless telephones](/wiki/Cordless_telephones "Cordless telephones") and [cell phones](/wiki/Cell_phones "Cell phones").[[19]](#cite_note-Gibson26-21)

## Technologies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Digital_audio&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Technologies")]

Digital audio is used in [broadcasting](/wiki/Broadcasting "Broadcasting") of audio. Standard technologies include [Digital audio broadcasting](/wiki/Digital_audio_broadcasting "Digital audio broadcasting") (DAB), [Digital Radio Mondiale](/wiki/Digital_Radio_Mondiale "Digital Radio Mondiale") (DRM), [HD Radio](/wiki/HD_Radio "HD Radio") and [In-band on-channel](/wiki/In-band_on-channel "In-band on-channel") (IBOC).

Digital audio in recording applications is stored on audio-specific technologies including CD, DAT, [Digital Compact Cassette](/wiki/Digital_Compact_Cassette "Digital Compact Cassette") (DCC) and [MiniDisc](/wiki/MiniDisc "MiniDisc"). Digital audio may be stored in a standard [audio file formats](/wiki/Audio_file_format "Audio file format") and stored on a [Hard disk recorder](/wiki/Hard_disk_recorder "Hard disk recorder"), [Blu-ray](/wiki/Blu-ray "Blu-ray") or [DVD-Audio](/wiki/DVD-Audio "DVD-Audio"). Files may be played back on smartphones, computers or [MP3 player](/wiki/MP3_player "MP3 player"). Digital audio resolution is measured in [audio bit depth](/wiki/Audio_bit_depth "Audio bit depth"). Most digital audio formats use either 16-bit, 24-bit, and 32-bit resolution.

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Digital_audio&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-4)** Anti-alias filtering and optional digital signal processing may degrade the audio signal via passband ripple, non-linear phase shift, numeric precision quantization noise or time distortion of transients. However, these potential degradations can be limited by careful digital design.[[3]](#cite_note-3)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-5)** Some audio signals, such as those created by [digital synthesis](/wiki/Synthesizer "Synthesizer"), originate entirely in the digital domain, in which case analog-to-digital conversion does not take place.
